section.title: God Renews His Promise
section.sectionRationale: God answers Moses' complaint from the end of chapter 5 with a fuller self-revelation and a sevenfold covenant promise, then the narrative pauses for a genealogy anchoring Moses and Aaron in Israel's tribal record before the confrontation with Pharaoh resumes.
section.thesis: When Moses despairs that nothing has changed, God answers not with an explanation but with Himself: I am the LORD, revealing a name Israel's fathers never fully knew and a sevenfold promise of deliverance, relationship, and inheritance.
section.frameNote: Three movements: God's promise, I am the LORD, and the sevenfold I will; Israel's unbelief from crushed spirits; the genealogy anchoring Moses and Aaron in Israel's family line.

verses.text: But the LORD said to Moses, “Now you will see what I will do to Pharaoh, for because of My mighty hand he will let the people go; because of My strong hand he will drive them out of his land.”
God also told Moses, “I am the {{yhwh2|LORD}}.
I appeared to Abraham, to Isaac, and to Jacob as {{elshaddai|God Almighty}}, but by My name the LORD I did not make Myself known to them.
I also established My covenant with them to give them the land of Canaan, the land where they lived as foreigners.
Furthermore, I have heard the groaning of the Israelites, whom the Egyptians are enslaving, and I have remembered My covenant.

Therefore tell the Israelites: ‘I am the LORD, and I will bring you out from under the yoke of the Egyptians and deliver you from their bondage. I will {{gaal|redeem}} you with an outstretched arm and with mighty acts of judgment.
I will take you as My own people, and I will be your God. Then you will know that I am the LORD your God, who brought you out from under the yoke of the Egyptians.
And I will bring you into the land that I swore to give to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob. I will give it to you as a possession. I am the LORD!’”
Moses relayed this message to the Israelites, but on account of their broken spirit and cruel bondage, they did not listen to him.
So the LORD said to Moses,
“Go and tell Pharaoh king of Egypt to let the Israelites go out of his land.”
But in the LORD's presence Moses replied, “If the Israelites will not listen to me, then why would Pharaoh listen to me, since I am unskilled in speech?”
Then the LORD spoke to Moses and Aaron and gave them a charge concerning both the Israelites and Pharaoh king of Egypt, to bring the Israelites out of the land of Egypt.
These were the heads of their fathers' houses: The sons of Reuben, the firstborn of Israel, were Hanoch and Pallu, Hezron and Carmi. These were the clans of Reuben.
The sons of Simeon were Jemuel, Jamin, Ohad, Jachin, Zohar, and Shaul, the son of a Canaanite woman. These were the clans of Simeon.
These were the names of the sons of Levi according to their records: Gershon, Kohath, and Merari. Levi lived 137 years.
The sons of Gershon were Libni and Shimei, by their clans.
The sons of Kohath were Amram, Izhar, Hebron, and Uzziel. Kohath lived 133 years.
The sons of Merari were Mahli and Mushi. These were the clans of the Levites according to their records.
And Amram married his father's sister Jochebed, and she bore him Aaron and Moses. Amram lived 137 years.
The sons of Izhar were Korah, Nepheg, and Zichri.
The sons of Uzziel were Mishael, Elzaphan, and Sithri.
And Aaron married Elisheba, the daughter of Amminadab and sister of Nahshon, and she bore him Nadab and Abihu, Eleazar and Ithamar.
The sons of Korah were Assir, Elkanah, and Abiasaph. These were the clans of the Korahites.
Aaron's son Eleazar married one of the daughters of Putiel, and she bore him Phinehas. These were the heads of the Levite families by their clans.
It was this Aaron and Moses to whom the LORD said, “Bring the Israelites out of the land of Egypt by their divisions.”
Moses and Aaron were the ones who spoke to Pharaoh king of Egypt in order to bring the Israelites out of Egypt.
Now on the day that the LORD spoke to Moses in Egypt,
He said to him, “I am the LORD; tell Pharaoh king of Egypt everything I say to you.”
But in the LORD's presence Moses replied, “Since I am unskilled in speech, why would Pharaoh listen to me?”

yhwh2.gloss: The LORD, God's covenant name first disclosed fully at the burning bush. Here God repeats I am the LORD three times, bracketing the whole promise, grounding every clause of deliverance in His own unchanging being.

elshaddai.gloss: God Almighty, the name by which God revealed Himself to the patriarchs. God now says that by His name the LORD He was not fully known to them, marking a deeper stage of revelation for the generation about to be redeemed.

gaal.gloss: To redeem, the action of a kinsman redeemer who buys back a relative from slavery or debt. God casts Himself as Israel's Redeemer, the first great use of a word that will echo through the rest of Scripture, culminating in Christ's redemption of sinners.

basic.context: Moses had just complained to God that things had gotten worse, not better, since he obeyed his call. God responds not with a rebuke but with a fuller revelation of His name and a sevenfold promise: He will bring Israel out, redeem them, take them as His people, and bring them into the promised land. The chapter then pauses for a genealogy confirming Moses and Aaron's place in Israel's family tree.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a straightforward historical account of God's covenant renewal, but it is theologically dense: the sevenfold I will statements form the backbone of redemption language throughout the rest of the Bible. The genealogy, though easy to skip, roots the whole account in real, verifiable family history.
basic.originalLanguages: God says I am YHWH three times in these verses, bracketing His promise in His own unchanging name. He distinguishes this name from El Shaddai, God Almighty, known to the patriarchs. And He promises to gaal, redeem, Israel, the language of a kinsman buying back a relative from bondage.
basic.typology: God's sevenfold I will promise here, bring out, deliver, redeem, take as My people, be your God, bring in, and give as a possession, becomes the pattern of redemption fulfilled and surpassed in Christ, who redeems not from Egypt but from sin, and brings His people not into Canaan but into eternal inheritance.
basic.christ: The redemption language here, gaal, redeem, with an outstretched arm and mighty acts of judgment, anticipates the greater redemption Paul describes in Christ, who redeemed us from the curse of the law, Galatians 3:13, by His own outstretched arms on the cross.
basic.god: God is the self-revealing Redeemer who keeps every word of His covenant, even when His people are too crushed to listen. He is patient with Moses' continued reluctance, answering doubt not with rejection but with repeated reassurance grounded in His own name.
basic.crossReferences: Genesis 17:1 shows God appearing to Abraham as El Shaddai. Galatians 3:13 has Christ redeeming us from the law's curse. 1 Peter 1:18-19 speaks of redemption not with silver or gold but with Christ's precious blood.
basic.oneStory: This is the theological heart of the exodus: God explains who He is and what He will do before He does it, and every clause of His sevenfold promise becomes a pattern repeated and fulfilled in the greater redemption accomplished by Christ.

context.literary: A direct divine speech answering Moses' complaint from 5:22-23, structured around a sevenfold I will, followed by the Israelites' unbelief and a genealogical interlude that anchors the narrative in verifiable family history before resuming the Pharaoh confrontation in chapter 7.
context.historical: The genealogy reflects standard ancient Near Eastern practice of establishing a leader's legitimacy through tribal descent, here tracing Moses and Aaron specifically through Levi, the priestly tribe, confirming their standing to speak for Israel and, later, to serve at the altar.
context.canonical: The sevenfold covenant promise here becomes foundational language echoed throughout Scripture, especially in the covenant formula I will be your God and you will be My people, repeated from Leviticus 26:12 to Revelation 21:3.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To ground Israel's coming deliverance in God's own self-revealed name and covenant faithfulness, not in Moses' ability or the people's belief, and to anchor the deliverers, Moses and Aaron, in Israel's verifiable family line.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage describes God's specific covenant promise to Israel regarding the exodus and the land; it is not a general promise of material deliverance to every believer today. The enduring principle is the pattern of redemption itself: God who redeems, claims a people, and brings them into inheritance.

debates.question: Did the patriarchs never use or hear the name YHWH before this?

views.label: The name was unknown in its full covenant significance
views.case: Genesis does use the name YHWH in narration and patriarchal speech, but Exodus 6:3 may mean the patriarchs did not experience the full weight of what the name would come to mean through the exodus deliverance.

views.label: The name itself was genuinely new information
views.case: Some read verse 3 more literally, that the specific name YHWH, as opposed to titles like El Shaddai, was not revealed to the patriarchs at all, with its use in Genesis reflecting the narrator's later perspective.

debates.bearing: Both views affirm the same central point: a new, fuller depth of God's covenant faithfulness is being unveiled precisely at the moment of Israel's redemption, whatever the precise linguistic history of the name.

study.originalLanguages: God's threefold I am YHWH frames the whole promise in His own unchanging name, distinguished from El Shaddai, known to the patriarchs. He promises to gaal, redeem, His people, the kinsman redeemer's action that becomes a central metaphor for salvation across all of Scripture.

typology.type: The sevenfold I will of covenant redemption
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled and deepened in Christ's redemption of believers from sin, and in the new covenant promise, I will be their God and they will be My people, Hebrews 8:10, fully realized in Revelation 21:3.

typology.type: God as Israel's kinsman Redeemer, gaal
typology.fulfillment: Christ, who redeems believers not with silver or gold but with His own precious blood, 1 Peter 1:18-19, the ultimate outstretched arm of redemption.

study.christ: The redemption language of this chapter, gaal, with an outstretched arm and mighty acts of judgment, sets the pattern the New Testament applies directly to Christ, who redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us, Galatians 3:13. The covenant formula I will take you as My own people, I will be your God, finds its ultimate fulfillment in the new covenant sealed by Christ's blood and consummated in the new creation, Revelation 21:3.
study.god: God is self-revealing and self-consistent, grounding every promise in His own unchanging name, I am the LORD. He is patient with unbelief, both Israel's crushed refusal to listen and Moses' continued self-doubt, answering both not with rejection but with repeated, gracious reassurance and a formal charge to proceed regardless of feeling ready.

crossReferences.note: God appeared to Abraham as El Shaddai, God Almighty, the name contrasted here with the fuller revelation of YHWH.
The covenant formula, I will be your God and you will be My people, first stated fully here, recurs throughout Scripture.
Christ redeems believers from the curse of the law, fulfilling the redemption, gaal, promised here.
The covenant formula reaches its final, eternal fulfillment in the new creation.

study.oneStory: This is the theological center of the exodus: God answers despair not with an explanation but with His own name and a sevenfold promise of redemption, relationship, and inheritance. That same pattern of redemption, an outstretched arm buying back the enslaved, reaches its fullest expression in Christ, who redeems His people not from Egypt but from sin and death.

sermon.title: My Name Is God Almighty
sermon.why: Piper preaches directly through Exodus 5:22-6:8, the exact hinge of this chapter, unpacking how God reminds Moses of both His name El Shaddai and His name YHWH to ground Israel's confidence in the omnipotence and covenant faithfulness of God during the darkest moment of the story.

quiz.q: How did God respond to Moses' complaint that things had gotten worse for Israel?

options.text: He revealed His name more fully and gave a sevenfold promise of deliverance, relationship, and inheritance.
options.note: Verses 2 to 8 record this direct, gracious, and detailed response to Moses' anguish.

options.text: He removed Moses from leadership.
options.note: God continues to charge Moses and Aaron with the mission in verse 13.

options.text: He remained silent and gave no answer.
options.note: God speaks at length and in detail in response to Moses' complaint.

options.text: He rebuked Moses harshly for doubting.
options.note: God's response is reassurance and promise, not rebuke.

quiz.q: What name did God say the patriarchs knew Him by, in contrast to the name LORD?

options.text: Adonai, my Lord.
options.note: This is a title of address, not the specific name the text contrasts with YHWH.

options.text: Elohim, simply God.
options.note: The text specifies El Shaddai as the contrasted name, not the general term Elohim.

options.text: El Shaddai, God Almighty.
options.note: Verse 3 states this explicitly, distinguishing the patriarchs' experience from the fuller revelation now given to Moses.

options.text: No other name is mentioned.
options.note: El Shaddai is explicitly named in verse 3.

quiz.q: Why did the Israelites not listen when Moses relayed God's promise?

options.text: Because of their broken spirit and cruel bondage.
options.note: Verse 9 gives this exact reason for their unbelief, showing suffering can deafen people even to good news.

options.text: Because they had already been freed and no longer needed the promise.
options.note: They were still enslaved; this is the opposite of their situation.

options.text: The text gives no reason for their response.
options.note: A specific reason, their broken spirit and cruel bondage, is given directly.

options.text: Because they did not trust Moses personally.
options.note: The stated cause is their crushed condition under bondage, not personal distrust of Moses.

quiz.q: What is the purpose of the genealogy in the second half of this chapter?

options.text: It has no purpose in the narrative.
options.note: It serves a clear purpose, establishing Moses and Aaron's family credentials.

options.text: To record the plagues that would come.
options.note: The plagues are recorded in later chapters, not in this genealogical section.

options.text: To list the kings of Egypt.
options.note: The genealogy is entirely about Israelite family lines, not Egyptian rulers.

options.text: To anchor Moses and Aaron in Israel's verifiable tribal and family history, especially the line of Levi.
options.note: Verses 14 to 25 trace descent through Levi specifically to establish Moses and Aaron's identity and standing.

quiz.q: What Hebrew concept lies behind God's promise, I will redeem you with an outstretched arm?

options.text: Shalom, meaning peace.
options.note: Shalom is a different Hebrew concept, not the redemption term used in this promise.

options.text: Gaal, the action of a kinsman redeemer buying back a relative from bondage or debt.
options.note: This is the term used in verse 6, foundational for redemption imagery throughout Scripture.

options.text: No Hebrew concept is behind this promise.
options.note: The specific Hebrew term gaal underlies this exact promise.

options.text: Torah, meaning instruction or law.
options.note: Torah refers to teaching or law, not the redemption imagery in this verse.

questions: God answered Moses' complaint not with an explanation of the delay but with a fuller revelation of who He is. When you are struggling with unanswered questions, how might focusing on God's character rather than demanding explanations change your prayers?
The Israelites were too crushed by suffering to hear good news. Is there an area of your life where pain has made it hard for you to receive God's promises, even when they are true and near?

God's sevenfold promise moves from rescue to relationship to inheritance. Which of these, being brought out of bondage, being claimed as God's own, or receiving an inheritance, do you most need to hold onto today?
Moses still felt unqualified even after God's fresh promise, describing himself as unskilled in speech. Where do you need to trust God's commission over your own sense of inadequacy?
God is called Israel's Redeemer, One who buys back what was lost. How does knowing Christ has redeemed you at great personal cost change how you see your own worth and your own bondage to sin?

translationNotes.note: The Hebrew phrase behind God Almighty is El Shaddai, whose precise etymology is debated among scholars, though its meaning of overwhelming power is well attested by its usage across Genesis and Job.
The phrase rendered broken spirit is literally shortness of spirit or breath, a Hebrew idiom for being crushed, impatient, or unable to bear more, vividly describing Israel's exhausted unbelief.
The phrase I am unskilled in speech is literally I have uncircumcised lips, a Hebrew idiom for being unfit or unable to speak effectively, repeated again by Moses in verse 30.
